module.title: Mastering
module.description: Understand what mastering does, how to prepare a mix for it, and how to build a master bus chain that makes your track competitive on streaming platforms — without destroying the dynamics that make it worth listening to.

lessons.title: What Mastering Is (and Isn't)
lessons.body:
Mastering is the final processing stage before a track is distributed. It prepares the mix for the widest possible range of playback environments — streaming services, vinyl, club systems, phones, car stereos — and ensures it sits at a consistent, competitive loudness relative to other tracks on the same platform.

Mastering is widely misunderstood. Producers often expect it to fix problems in the mix, or to add 'something' that was missing. Understanding what mastering can and cannot do prevents wasted effort and sets realistic expectations.

## What Mastering Can Do

**Optimise loudness**: Bring the track to a loudness level appropriate for streaming distribution (typically around −14 LUFS integrated) using a combination of compression and limiting.

**Correct broad tonal balance**: If a mix is slightly dark, slightly harsh, or slightly heavy in the low-mids, mastering EQ can make small corrective adjustments to the overall spectral balance.

**Enhance stereo width**: Mid-side processing on the master can subtly widen the stereo image or clean up phase issues in the low end.

**Ensure consistency across an album**: When mastering a collection of tracks, the mastering stage aligns loudness, tonal character, and spacing between tracks for a cohesive listening experience.

**Protect against clipping**: The master limiter ensures no sample exceeds the digital ceiling during distribution and format conversion.

## What Mastering Cannot Do

**Fix a bad mix**: Mastering applies processing to the full stereo mix — two channels of audio. It cannot reach inside that stereo file and separately adjust the kick, the bass, or the vocals. A kick that is too quiet in the mix is too quiet in the master; the only fix is returning to the mix.

**Add elements that are not there**: If the mix lacks excitement, punch, or clarity, mastering cannot create those qualities from nothing. It can only enhance what is present.

**Correct phase problems**: If tracks in the mix are cancelling each other due to phase issues, the master receives that cancellation baked into the stereo file. Mastering cannot undo it.

**Make a bad song sound good**: Mastering improves the technical presentation of a mix. It does not improve the composition, arrangement, or performances.

## The Mix Is the Foundation

The most important mastering principle: **a great mix needs minimal mastering**. A well-gain-staged, well-balanced mix with clean low end, controlled dynamics, and proper headroom should reach near-competitive loudness with just a limiter. When a master bus chain requires aggressive EQ, heavy compression, and hard limiting to sound acceptable, the mix has problems that should be solved in the mix.

**The headroom rule**: Leave −3 to −6 dBFS of headroom on the master before any limiting. This gives the mastering chain room to work without clipping on input. If your master peaks at −1 dBFS before the limiter, the limiter is doing emergency clipping prevention rather than musical loudness shaping.

## Self-Mastering vs Professional Mastering

For most independent producers releasing to streaming platforms, self-mastering is entirely viable if the mix is solid. The tools available in FL Studio and free plugins are sufficient to produce master-ready audio.

Professional mastering is valuable when:
- The tracks will be released on vinyl (requires specific EQ and phase corrections)
- An album needs consistency across many tracks with different mixes
- The mix was done on inaccurate monitors and needs an objective outside listen
- The track is for sync licensing or broadcast and must meet specific technical standards

For most electronic music and hip-hop released digitally, a careful self-master on accurate headphones or monitors is the practical choice.
keyTakeaways: Mastering cannot fix mix problems — it processes the full stereo file as two channels and cannot reach inside to adjust individual elements. A bad mix produces a bad master regardless of how much processing is applied.
Leave −3 to −6 dBFS of headroom on the master output before any limiting — a mix peaking at −1 dBFS has no room for a mastering chain to work musically.

A great mix needs minimal mastering: a well-balanced mix with clean low end and proper headroom should need only a limiter and perhaps one or two small EQ moves to reach competitive streaming loudness.
lessons.tryThis: Export a full mix WAV from your current project without any master bus processing. Open the file in Edison or check the peak level in the export dialog. If the peak is above −3 dBFS, go back to your mix and reduce levels until the pre-master export peaks at −6 dBFS or lower. This is the starting point for mastering — and the exercise often reveals gain staging problems in the mix that you did not notice at mix volume. Fix those problems in the mix before adding any master bus processing.

lessons.title: Preparing Your Mix for Mastering
lessons.body:
The difference between a mix that masters cleanly in 20 minutes and one that requires an hour of troubleshooting is almost always preparation. A properly prepared mix gives the mastering chain predictable, consistent input and requires only enhancement rather than correction. This lesson covers every preparation step before you touch the master bus.

## Step 1: Remove All Master Bus Processing

Before preparing a mix for mastering, remove or bypass everything on the master bus — limiters, compressors, EQ, saturators. You need to hear the raw mix to evaluate it accurately and give yourself headroom for the mastering chain.

Common mistake: mixing with a limiter on the master to achieve a loud, competitive sound while mixing. This masks problems, encourages poor gain staging, and leaves no headroom for the actual mastering stage. Mix without a master limiter. Add it only at the mastering stage.

## Step 2: Gain Staging Audit

With master bus processing removed, check that the mix peaks at −3 to −6 dBFS on the master meter. Play through the loudest section of the track:

- If the master peaks above −3 dBFS: reduce levels across the mix. The quickest fix is to lower the master fader or add −3 dB of output gain on the master bus. The better fix is to revisit individual track and bus levels.
- If the master peaks below −12 dBFS: the mix is very quiet. Check if tracks are under-levelled or if there is an issue with bus routing.

Target: the loudest section of the track peaks at approximately −4 to −6 dBFS with no master bus processing.

## Step 3: Low-End Mono Check

Add Fruity Stereo Enhancer to the master with Width set to 0 (mono). Play the full mix:

- Does the kick still hit clearly?
- Does the bass stay audible and present?
- Do any elements disappear completely?

Elements disappearing in mono indicate phase cancellation — two signals that are out of phase with each other and cancel when summed to mono. Common causes: excessive stereo chorus or flanger on bass, out-of-phase sample layering, or heavy stereo width on low-frequency content.

Fix mono-failing elements in the mix before mastering. The master cannot repair phase cancellation.

## Step 4: Reference Check

Load a commercial reference track at matched loudness and compare your mix to it:

- **Low end**: Is your kick and bass weight in the same range as the reference?
- **Mids**: Does your mix have similar presence and clarity in the 500 Hz–3 kHz range?
- **Highs**: Are your highs equally open, or dull, or harsh compared to the reference?
- **Overall energy**: Does the track feel as alive and dynamic as the reference?

Note specific differences — these are the targets for your mastering EQ and dynamics moves.

## Step 5: Check the Arrangement Tails

Play the very end of the track past the last note. Ensure:
- No reverb tails are cut off abruptly by the export region ending
- No automation clips leave parameters at unexpected values at the end
- The final decay fades to silence cleanly — no pops, clicks, or audio artefacts

Extend your export region by 2–4 bars beyond the last audible content to capture full reverb and delay tails.

## Step 6: Export the Pre-Master Mix

Export the fully prepared mix — without master bus processing — as a 32-bit float WAV at the project's sample rate (usually 44.1 kHz or 48 kHz). The 32-bit float format preserves full dynamic range even at levels above 0 dBFS internally, giving maximum quality for the mastering stage.

In FL Studio: **File → Export → WAV**, set quality to **32 Bit float**, ensure the export region covers the full track plus tail. This is the file you master.

## Working on Your Own Mix vs a Separate Master Session

For self-mastering, you can master directly in the same FL Studio project by processing the master bus after mixing. However, mastering in a separate session (importing the exported WAV into a new project) has advantages:

- Fresh ears without the memory of mixing decisions influencing judgement
- A clean signal path focused entirely on the master bus chain
- Easier to compare before and after at the full-track level
keyTakeaways: Remove all master bus processing before preparing the mix — mixing through a limiter masks gain staging problems and leaves no headroom for mastering. Mix raw, master separately.
Check the mix in mono before mastering: elements that disappear indicate phase cancellation that cannot be fixed at the mastering stage and must be addressed in the mix.
Export the pre-master mix as 32-bit float WAV — this format preserves full dynamic range regardless of levels, giving the mastering chain maximum quality to work with.
lessons.tryThis: Bypass everything on your master bus. Play the loudest section of your track and note the peak level on the master meter. If it exceeds −3 dBFS, reduce your master fader until the peak sits between −4 and −6 dBFS. Now add Fruity Stereo Enhancer at Width 0 and play the full mix in mono — note which elements feel weaker or disappear. Return those elements to their individual tracks and check for phase issues (out-of-phase layers, excessive stereo effects on bass). Fix any problems before continuing. This preparation step alone resolves the majority of mastering-stage problems.
